title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Back to nature Duo bring community agricultural project to Ester The Associated Press (Published June 25, 2001) Ester -- An experiment in community agriculture is taking shape in a patch of woods along the Old Nenana Highway. Susan Willsrud and Tom Zimmer, founders of Calypso Farm and Ecology Center, say their project will give people a chance to work on the farm in exchange for a share of the harvest. The concept is called "community shared agriculture." There are more than 1,000 such farms in the Lower 48, they say. "The key is to connect people to food sources," Zimmer said, "so they can see how it's grown and participate in it." Clearing has begun at the site to prepare for a substantial vegetable garden next year. The Calypso farm grew out of grants from the Alaska Conservation Fo…

title: Incentives promote oil from Alaska
summary: Washington -- In a broad plan to address the nation's brewing energy problems Thursday, President Bush proposed measures of conservation, research and a pipeline full of new oil and gas production from Alaska. President Bush's speech on energy • Listen to an audio recording of the entire speech • Watch video of Bush speaking about energy and the environment • Watch video of the Democrats' reaction to the address You will need RealPlayer to play these clips. A free version is available here . Clips courtesy of Minneapolis Star-Tribune. As envisioned under the Bush plan, the state's North Slope could be the nation's most important area of new oil and gas supply in coming years. For Alaska, the plan Bush announced at a Minnesota power plant contains the expected proposal to open the Arctic Na…

title: Dimond pool hall goes private to allow smoking
summary: When the doors open at Hot Shots Billiards each morning, no one is waiting to get in. Even on a Friday night, there's often a table free. The people who do come don't spend like the old crowd. The smoking crowd. Hot Shots Billiards owner Jim Foss says his revenue has declined by a third since the ban on smoking in most public places took effect in January. (Photo by Jim Lavrakas / Anchorage Daily News) So Hot Shots is taking down the no-smoking signs and setting out the ashtrays. Starting Saturday, it's declaring itself a private, members-only billiards club where smokers will be welcome. "They play pool. They smoke. The two seem to go together," said owner Jim Foss. His efforts to lure nonsmokers with family deals, a new menu, guys' nights, girls' nights and other discounts haven't made u…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Chemical facility planned in Valley CONCERN: Borough officials worried about proposal to make chlorine. By S.J. Komarnitsky Anchorage Daily News (Published June 8, 2001) Wasilla -- A proposal to locate a chemical plant in a populated area has alarmed Mat-Su Borough officials. An enterprise identifying itself as Chlorine Industry Inc. submitted a request in March to the state fire marshal to build a plant just off the Palmer-Wasilla Highway that could include storing up to six tons of chlorine gas. Chlorine is highly toxic and reacts with moisture. It can cause severe burns and in high concentrations can asphyxiate people. It is also denser than air, which means if it leaked out, the gas would likely stay close to the ground, said Kevin Koechlein, who heads the Mat-Su Borough Department…

title: Two drown in separate boating accidents
summary: Two tourists drawn to Alaska by the state's wild water died in separate boating accidents, Alaska State Troopers reported Tuesday. Michel Saint Andre, a 55-year-old teacher from the French city of Clermont-Ferrand, drowned Monday in the Talachulitna River, a Susitna River tributary. The raft carrying Andre and two friends flipped on a log spanning the snowmelt-swollen river. Also on Monday, a pilot spotted the word "help" spelled out on a blue tarp on the Charley River about 60 miles southeast of Circle. When troopers investigated, they located a rafting party who told them that George Zeiter, 41, of Sylvania, Ohio, drowned Friday in the boulder-strewn river. High, fast water flipped the canoes and inflatable kayaks paddled by Zeiter and five factory buddies from a Chrysler plant. The acci…

title: Wuerch orders gay pride exhibit from library
summary: A gay pride exhibit was abruptly removed from a display wall at Z.J. Loussac Public Library on Tuesday after Mayor George Wuerch objected to the installation. The third-floor display, including posters and a large, rainbow banner saying "Celebrate Diversity under the midnight sun," went up Monday evening and came down before the library's 10 a.m. opening Tuesday. No one had complained. The mayor wouldn't elaborate on why he disapproved. He was unaware the exhibit was already in place, but said had he known, he would have ordered it down. "I just said 'no,' " Wuerch said. "This is a fundamental responsibility of the mayor, to make decisions. Once in a while, you have to say no." The exhibit was sponsored by Metropolitan Community Church, a predominately gay and lesbian congregation, and Par…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Chugach Alaska rebounds PROFITS: Corporation brass to split more than $1 million in bonuses. By Paula Dobbyn Anchorage Daily News (Published May 30, 2001) Eight members of Chugach Alaska Corp.'s board of directors will receive bonuses of around $10,000 each based on an "extraordinary" year in which in the Alaska Native corporation more than doubled its profits, according to chairman Sheri Buretta. In addition to the board, about 50 top managers will split the rest of a bonus incentive pool worth $1 million to $1.3 million, Buretta said. The Anchorage-based regional corporation, mired until recently in federal Bankruptcy Court while it reorganized, has made key strides in government services contracting, Buretta said. Although an auditing firm is still crunching final numbers for 2000, …

title: Garbage-grubbing bruin sparks Afognak fire
summary: It's a rare camper who hasn't heard Smokey Bear's message: "Only you can prevent forest fires!" Some of his species on Afognak Island apparently aren't so concerned. Bears are blamed for a four-acre fire Monday night at a Danger Bay logging camp. An Afognak Native Association fire truck and a crew from the logging camp extinguished the fire, which caused little damage in the logged-over area but has provided some chuckles for forestry workers who usually must cope with human-caused blazes. "They were burning some of the trash and evidently some bears came along and decided to dig around in it," said Andy Williams of the Alaska Interagency Fire Information Center. The bears tossed some of the burning debris onto the ground and sparked the fire. "I've heard of fires being caused by animals n…

title: Gas-fired future? With prices up, Alaska's gas dream edges toward reality
summary: Brooks Range -- John Moran hates the cold, but he loves the North Slope. In particular, the Houston, Texas, resident and geologist is fond of a low ridge that sticks from the Brooks Range like a hitchhiker's thumb. At Moran's company, Anadarko, the exploration area is known as K2. Moran calls it "potentially world class." Anadarko geologist John Moran is helping his company search for natural gas in the foothills of the Brooks Range. (Photo by Erik Hill / Anchorage Daily News) But there is a twist. Unlike the past 40 years of North Slope exploration, Anadarko is not after oil. In the first effort of its kind, the company is searching for natural gas. Right now, there is no way to get North Slope natural gas to market. More than 2,000 miles from the existing pipeline grid in Canada and frei…

title: BACKPACKINGBackpacker offers tips for women who hit the trail
summary: BACKPACKING Backpacker offers tips for women who hit the trail Seasoned backpacker Susan Dunsmore offers tips for women looking to put their home on their back and hit the trail. In a free clinic at Recreational Equipment Inc. at 7 p.m. Tuesday, she'll discuss gear selection, clothing, equipment checklists, trip planning, safety and where to go. For information, call 272-4565. BIKING Arctic Bicycle Club plans Hillside, Bicentennial ride The mountain bike trails of Hillside and Bicentennial parks are open, and the touring division of the Arctic Bicycle Club plans to hit one of them Tuesday. The club will ride at 7 p.m. out of the Abbott Loop parking lot access to Hillside. The plan is to give the legs and lungs a workout by pedaling up and around the Spencer Loop. For more information, call…

title: Corrosion is constant enemy
summary: Just past 11 p.m. last Sunday, Phillips Alaska's Kuparuk field operations manager, Bill Patterson, got the call an oil executive dreads: The field had a spill. Other recent stories about ANWR: • ENERGY: Murkowski • Norton remains firm on ANWR • Toohey's appointment sparks cry of conflict in House • Secretary touts ANWR potential • Murkowski steps off swinging • More ANWR stories • ANWR facts and figures A pipeline had ruptured, spilling a hot mixture of salt water and crude onto the tundra. At 92,400 gallons, the spill may be the largest ever to hit the North Slope's fragile tundra. The next day Patterson flew to Kuparuk, which sits west of Prudhoe Bay and is the Slope's second-largest oil field. Within 24 hours, most of the water and crude had been recovered. But the damage had been done.…

title: Inside Alaska Business
summary: Gas leak shuts down line During routine maintenance on the 800-mile trans-Alaska oil pipeline workers discovered a gas leak in a valve at Pump Station 4, the Alyeska Pipeline Service Co. said Wednesday. Efforts to repair the leak have been unsuccessful, according to Alyeska. Workers shut down the gas line at the pump and removed the valve for repair. The gas line will be isolated until a scheduled maintenance shutdown in September, when the repaired valve will be re-installed. Pump Station 4 sits in the northern foothills of the Brooks Range. Two companies begin taking plastics As Smurfit Stone Recycling begins accepting some plastics today, Girmscheid Enterprises Curbside Recycling is dropping glass and adding plastics to its pickup service, which is available to businesses and homes. The…

title: Cruise bill set to sail
summary: Juneau -- Deep in the bowels of the Statendam, many floors below the dining room and dance floor, the beauty salon and the spa, wastewater from sinks, showers and toilets runs through a maze of pipes and treatment tanks. It's an expensive maze. The new treatment systems, which Holland America Line has installed on two of its six ships, cost $2.5 million apiece. Holland America, like the eight other major cruise lines that ply Alaska's waters each summer, is preparing for new cruise ship pollution legislation, which is expected to pass in a special session that Gov. Tony Knowles has called for Thursday. The trend toward tougher environmental standards is occurring worldwide, said Richard Softye, Holland America's vice president for compliance programs. "We saw it coming down the line, and w…

title: Sea lion evidence disputed
summary: "There is great uncertainty about the effects of the groundfish fisheries on Steller sea lions, and the evidence presented so far is almost entirely circumstantial." That's the conclusion of a report by independent scientists after reviewing the Biological Opinion (BiOp) that was prepared by the National Marine Fisheries Service last November. The group was contracted by the North Pacific Fishery Management Council to review the controversial opinion, which many have criticized for its "questionable science." Regardless, the BiOp resulted in a finding of jeopardy to the western stock of sea lions and set forth federal management measures (dubbed Reasonable and Prudent Alternatives, or RPAs) intended to protect the endangered marine mammals. Since then, boats targeting pollock, Pacific cod …

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Kepler-Bradley trails great for biking; let's make it legal By Melissa Devaughn (Published June 17, 2001) I spent a glorious sunny afternoon breaking the law last Sunday. A group of us from the Women's Mountain Bike and Tea Society gathered at the Kepler-Bradley State Recreation Area and pedaled a single-track trail so wonderfully narrow it often disappears beneath the undergrowth. Jeanne Higgins grinds up a steep hill in the Kepler Bradley State Recreation Area. The park is currently off limits for mountain bikers, but members of the Women's Mountain Biking and Tea Society had special permission for a ride.(Photo by Anne Raup / Anchorage Daily News) The area is one of the best places to ride in Southcentral Alaska. Trails skirt Matanuska, Long, Kepler, Silver and Victor lakes, among o…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Field Notes (Published June 17, 2001) FISHING Deshka River opens for bait fishing The Deshka River, one of the best king salmon streams in the Susitna Valley, opened to bait fishing on Tuesday from the mouth to about 17 miles upstream at an Alaska Department of Fish and Game marker at Chijuk Creek. A closure remains, however, around the fish-counting weir at river mile 7. Almost 6,000 kings had been counted through that weir by the start of the week, and biologists were projecting a return about 25 percent greater than the biological escapement goal of 17,500 fish. CYCLING Mountain bikers head to Peters Creek Mountain bikers in the Arctic Bicycle Club head for the Peters Creek Trail in Chugach State Park Tuesday evening. The trail climbs up into the mountains above Chugiak. Because of …

title: Native rape victim finds self-expression and healing in mixed media
summary: If someone hadn't raped artist Susie Silook, Grant Hall might stand empty tonight. Instead, it will bustle with the opening of a bold art show/happening titled "Ceremony of Healing." "I am the stereotype," artist Susie Silook says. "I am that drunken Native woman who got sexually assaulted." Silook has turned her anger into art. (Evan R. Steinhauser / Anchorage Daily News) Had authorities identified a suspect, Silook might not have produced work that screams out so explosively. "All the Rage" is a fanged, howling female. Her beaded eyes glare wildly. One raised hand holds an ulu, symbol of Eskimo womanhood; the other flashes claws and a bloody cross. Paint balls blotch her body. Had the assault been an isolated event, the show would be a solo exhibit. But it has drawn together a formidable…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Stevens defends drilling ANWR: Senator rips environmentalists, alleging they spread misinformation. By Ben Spiess Anchorage Daily News (Published May 22, 2001) Sen. Ted Stevens lashed out at opponents of drilling in the Arctic National Wildlife Refuge on Monday, accusing environmentalists of spreading misinformation about development in the refuge's coastal plain. The Alaska senator responded with some pro-drilling information of his own, saying that proposed alternative energy sources such wind, solar or corn-based fuel known as ethanol are not viable substitutes for refuge oil. "We've not faced up to take on the environmental community with their assertions of artificial supply," Stevens said as he rattled off statistics from the Department of Energy. To produce the same amount of en…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Temporarily permanent Economist looks at efforts to make resource wealth last By Bill White Anchorage Daily News (Published May 13, 2001) Here's the way it usually works: a government, handed the bounty from natural resources within its borders, spends the money as fast as it flows in. Alaska, through its oil-wealth Permanent Fund savings account, is among a small cadre of places that tried to sock away some of today's wealth for another day. A Norway-based economist recently studied the savings attempts by Alaska and three other places: Alberta, Norway and a tiny Pacific island nation called Nauru that was rich in phosphate. The economist, Rognvaldur Hannesson, explored a decades-old model devised by Nobel laureate Sir John Hicks that shows how temporary mineral wealth -- particularly…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Idaho court case may affect Tongass FORESTS: Bush administration faces deadline to weigh in on Clinton-imposed logging ban. By Paula Dobbyn Anchorage Daily News (Published May 4, 2001) Court papers to be filed in Idaho today will have a big impact on the future of logging and wildlife in Alaska's Tongass National Forest, according to environmentalists and timber industry representatives. That's because the Bush administration faces a court deadline today to reveal whether it supports or opposes a Clinton-era rule that would ban logging on about a third of national forestland, about 60 million acres nationwide, including 9.4 million in the Tongass. The Southeast forest is the country's largest national forest and has the most acres without roads. A spokesman for the U.S. Agriculture Dep…

title: Wuerch rethinks gay exhibit
summary: A day after he decided against a gay pride exhibit at the city library, Mayor George Wuerch said Wednesday he is rethinking his directive and will personally inspect the display materials before making a final call. The third-floor exhibit, sponsored by Metropolitan Community Church and Parents, Families and Friends of Lesbians and Gays, was put up Monday evening and removed Tuesday morning soon after Wuerch was briefed about it. He was shown a diagram but didn't see the exhibit. The exhibit, with posters and a rainbow-colored banner that said "Celebrate Diversity," may land in another city building if not Z.J. Loussac Public Library, Wuerch told reporters Wednesday. Nothing in the display was explicitly sexual. "The dialogue has been opened, and I've made a commitment to go back and re-ex…

title: Newspaper to widen Web reach
summary: The Anchorage Daily News has bought the Internet domain Alaska.com for an undisclosed price from General Communication Inc., which will partner with the paper to offer a wide-ranging Web site dedicated to Alaska. The newspaper, which announced the deal Tuesday, said the move builds on its effort to expand on the Internet, where it already offers the news site, adn.com. But Alaska.com thrusts the Daily News into a much a broader arena, where news coverage and advertising aren't everything. "The vision is to have a site that fulfills every need of every person in the state, as well as provide information for people wanting to visit or move here," said Sabrina Willis, new media manager at the Daily News. Willis wants people to click on Alaska.com to find information about everything from glac…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Agencies study Alaska salmon insurance plan By Laine Welch (Published May 5, 2001) Kodiak -- Farmers have a long history of being able to insure their crops against losses caused by drought, insects or other calamities, but commercial fishermen who face poor harvests have never been included in any insurance plans. That could soon change. Last year, Sen. Ted Stevens, R-Alaska, got salmon officially designated as a harvested food crop. That meant a salmon provision was tagged onto crop insurance, and a $1 million federal subsidy was designated for fishermen to insure their catches. Now, to figure out how crop insurance might be applied to fisheries, scoping meetings are being launched this month by University of Alaska Fairbanks researchers and U.S. Department of Agriculture risk manage…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Shipyard mulls vote on union DECISION: Alaska Ship and Dry Dock workers seek August election. The Associated Press (Published May 30, 2001) Ketchikan -- Workers at Alaska Ship and Dry Dock could be unionized soon. Piledrivers and Divers Local 2520 is looking to schedule a vote in August on whether to form a union at the Ketchikan shipyard. About a dozen shipyard employees, mostly welders, approached the union about a year ago, said Jim Strassburg, a union organizer. Since then, interest has grown among the work force, he said. Shipyard management says a union isn't needed. But Strassburg doesn't agree. "In all my years in Alaska working, if there is any (employees) that needed a union it is these people," he said. "One guy is working for $12 an hour, the next guy is working for $15 an …

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | USGS dismisses worker over ANWR caribou map 'ORWELLIAN': Agency says staffer put old data on Web at inconvenient time. By Lisa Getter Los Angeles Times (Published March 17, 2001) Washington -- Last week, Ian Thomas posted a map on a U.S. government Web site of the caribou calving areas in the Arctic National Wildlife Refuge, an area the Bush administration wants to open up for oil exploration. Now Thomas is looking for a new job. "I'm really flabbergasted," Thomas said this week. "After putting out 20,000 maps with no problem and then putting out one where baby caribou like to hang out, I got fired." Thomas, a contract employee for the U.S. Geological Survey's Patuxent Wildlife Research Center in Maryland, says he is a victim of politics. His offense: drawing attention to wildlife that…

title: Soldiers celebrate without berets
summary: Soldiers and officers at Fort Richardson were supposed to celebrate the Army's 226th anniversary Thursday with the official donning of a new part of their uniform, the black beret. They didn't. A series of production delays and political mix-ups have pushed the berets' expected arrival back to mid-September. But that might just be enough time for people to get ready for the controversial new headgear. Retired Sgt. 1st Class Scott Lehmkuh, relaxing around the barbecue with other active and retired Army personnel and their families at Fort Richardson's Cottonwood Park, said the new berets are the "right idea but the wrong color." That's because black berets used to be the official headgear of the Army Rangers. Rangers, along with Airborne troops that wear maroon berets and Special Forces, or…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Blowin' in the, Wind Once popular, Alaska Sailing Club looks for new members By Natalie Phillips Anchorage Daily News (Published June 17, 2001) BIG LAKE -- The sun was shining and temperatures hovered in the low 70s. For motorboats zipping around the lake, this was terrific news -- but not for the Big Lake yacht club. Past winners of the Governor's Cup include, from left, Paul Willing, Ben Robar, Birgitt Robar and Dave Johnson. Johnson's daughter, Michelle, is at right.(Photo by Evan Steinhauser / Anchorage Daily News) When the sun beams, the wind dies. "It usually means we have to wait for the evening for it to kick up," said Dave Johnson, past president of the Alaska Sailing Club. So on a stretch of Big Lake's southwest shore, a handful of club members toiled -- hammering on dock rep…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Hillside homeowners must stop baiting bears By Craig Medred Daily News Outdoors Editor (Published June 17, 2001) Hillside homeowners were baiting bears again the other night. Everywhere from Goldenview Drive north to Rabbit Creek Road and south to the Old Seward Highway, the smell of garbage was in the air. For bears, both black and brown, the scent of table scraps and rotten fruit is a temptation difficult to resist. A black bear tries to get into a grease barrel on Elmendorf Air Force Base. Bears, both black and brown, look for easy feeding opportunities. For many Anchorage Hillside bears, that means garbage cans put outside by homeowners. Many homeowners have a habit of placing their garbage cans at the curbside the night before it is scheduled to be picked up. (Photo by Fran Durner…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Cache for college Alaska to introduce 529 educational savings plan By Sarana Schell Anchorage Daily News (Published May 28, 2001) Alaska is jumping on board the latest trend in college savings strategies by introducing a 529 plan in the next few weeks. The plan provides more tax shelter than traditional educational savings options and could be a boon for certain investors. Depending on the scenario though, a Uniform Gift to a Minor Account, or UGMA, an option many parents use to save for college, is competitive with a 529 plan. "From a tax savings point of view there may not be that much difference, so the ownership rules and investment flexibility may be more important considerations," said Sam Beardsley, vice president at mutual fund company T. Rowe Price. Financial aid eligibility i…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Katie John appeal puts heat on Knowles DECISION: Governor risks alienating Native community if case goes the Supreme Court. By Martha Bellisle Anchorage Daily News (Published June 16, 2001) Juneau -- Last year, Gov. Tony Knowles said it was his "clear responsibility, even in the face of a difficult political battle," to appeal the Katie John subsistence decision, a position that angered the Democratic governor's supporters in Native communities and won praise from sport hunting groups and most Republican legislators. Now, with an appeals court having ordered the federal government to protect rural subsistence rights in Alaska to the delight of Native groups, Knowles once again must decide whether to continue the case, this time to the Supreme Court. As Knowles weighs his options, he's …

title: North Star wins loan from AIDEA
summary: A state development agency has approved a $9.4 million loan to help North Star Behavioral Health System reorganize its Anchorage hospital and treatment center under local ownership. That contribution from the Alaska Industrial and Development Export Authority is part of $11.7 million in loans to companies formed by North Star's chief executive and medical director, who plan to buy the two centers next month. Northrim Bank is the other lender. AIDEA, which tries to spark economic development in Alaska through financing business ventures, approved the loan at its board meeting last week. Tennessee-based The Healthcare Co. currently owns North Star Residential Treatment Center, a 30-bed residential treatment center on Bragaw Street. Maryland-based Magellan Health Services Inc. owns North Star…

title: Norton remains firm on ANWR
summary: On the last day of her second Alaska trip this year, Interior Secretary Gale Norton said that she had listened and understood the concerns of the Gwich'in people of Arctic Village but would not waver from her support for drilling in the Arctic National Wildlife Refuge. Other recent stories about ANWR: • Norton remains firm on ANWR • Toohey's appointment sparks cry of conflict in House • Secretary touts ANWR potential • Murkowski steps off swinging • Budget item creates ANWR stir • More ANWR stories • ANWR facts and figures Norton suggested Wednesday that Arctic Village should broaden its world view. By continuing to oppose ANWR drilling, she said, Arctic Village is affecting the future of other children, from Kaktovik, where people want drilling, to the Lower 48, where people need oil to h…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Inside Alaska Business (Published June 21, 2001) Mine closed for most of summer Construction projects at the Independence Mine State Historical Park at Hatcher Pass will keep the area's mining camp and visitor center closed for the majority of the summer, according to a Department of Natural Resources statement. There will be no historical building tours, and day-use parking fees will not be charged. Workers will be removing and replacing the viewing platforms at the water tunnel, repairing the tunnel entrance, maintaining trails throughout the mining camp, making the visitor center entrance accessible for the disabled, and improving roads, parking areas and access trails. Hatcher Pass Road is also being paved, causing delays of up to 20 minutes or more. Call the Department of Transpor…

title: When bear attacked, Alaskan fought back
summary: Already bloodied by a protective sow grizzly bear, 47-year-old Gary Titus found himself getting angry about the animal's decision to drop him and charge uphill after hiking companion Ellen Snoeyenbos. "I was mad," he said Friday from a bed at Alaska Regional Hospital in Anchorage. "When that bear dropped me and went after Ellen, I was mad." Titus understood why the bear attacked. The historian for the Kenai National Wildlife Refuge is a backcountry ranger and experienced woodsman, familiar with the protective nature of brown bears. "It's defending its cub," Titus said. "You can't blame a bear for defending a cub. I don't want this made out like the bear was bad." He and Snoeyenbos had stumbled on the sow Wednesday while returning from a climb to the summit of an unnamed mountain near Twin …

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Stranded dipnetters evacuated AVALANCHE: The salmon fishermen were trapped for about 36 hours. By Criag Medred And Doug O'harra Anchorage Daily News (Published June 19, 2001) Wood Canyon -- The heroes drove heavy equipment on Monday. Despite the rotten cliffs of the Copper River, raining down rock and mud and ice, equipment operators punched open the rugged Copper River Road on Monday afternoon to free 200 to 300 salmon dipnetters trapped by a landslide a day and a half earlier. But even as grateful fishermen with their coolers of salmon rolled out from the canyon, the slide dumped debris on the newly plowed route. Alaska State Troopers and the state Department of Transportation ordered everyone to evacuate north of O'Brien Creek. Transportation district manager George Lavasseuer said …

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Alaskans warm to lure of Antarctica jobs NANA: Firm recruiting 125 people to fill camp services contract. By Paula Dobbyn Anchorage Daily News (Published June 20, 2001) The lure of Antarctica drew a steady stream of adventure-seekers to a job fair in Anchorage on Tuesday. Kim Givens, who has worked the last 11 summers in Antarctica, answers questions at Tuesday's job fair. (Photo by Bill Roth / Anchorage Daily News) NANA Services is recruiting 125 people to work in three camps on the icy continent from October to February, when it's spring and summer there. NANA, a subsidiary of a regional Native corporation, has a 10-year, $5.5 million contract with Raytheon Polar Services to provide food, housecleaning and other support services. Raytheon has its own contract with the National Scienc…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Kodiak fish plant hauls in big fine GUILTY: International Seafoods agrees to pay $196,000 for underreporting pollock purchases. By Wesley Loy Anchorage Daily News (Published June 16, 2001) A Kodiak fish-processing company pleaded guilty Friday to a federal felony charge of illegally underreporting the amount of pollock purchased from a fishing boat. International Seafoods of Alaska Inc., whose plant is one of the biggest in the commercial fishing hub of Kodiak, was fined $150,000 and put on probation for five years under a plea agreement with prosecutors. The company also must pay another $46,000, the estimated market value of pollock illegally received at the plant in the first three months of 1999. And it must run ads in the Maine-based trade journal National Fisherman acknowledging …

title: Poll: Most favor Wuerch's library decision
summary: A new opinion poll suggests that a majority of Anchorage residents support Mayor George Wuerch's decision last week not to allow a gay pride exhibit at Loussac Library. The telephone poll of 393 voters found that 54 percent approved of his decision, compared with 39 percent who disapproved. The other 7 percent weren't sure. The survey was conducted Tuesday and Wednesday by Ivan Moore Research and was commissioned by the Daily News, KTUU-Channel 2 and KFQD-750 AM. It has an error margin of 4.95 percent. The contrast of opinions of Wuerch's decision was much sharper when the poll respondents are sorted by ideology. Of those who identified themselves as conservative, 86 percent said they approved of the mayor's action and just 8 percent disapproved. For those who considered themselves progres…

title: GAY PRIDE: Civil liberties union, others want exhibit back at Loussac.
summary: Saying the city wrongly removed a gay pride exhibit from Z.J. Loussac Public Library, the Alaska Civil Liberties Union on Wednesday sued to have it immediately reinstated and to permanently bar the city from discriminating against other displays. The exhibit came down, the lawsuit says, "because of Municipal officials' disapproval or discomfort with the actual or perceived ideas expressed in the exhibit." That violates the 32 named plaintiffs' rights to freedom of speech and to receive information free of censorship under the U.S. and Alaska constitutions, the suit against the city says. "We want it up. We want it up now. We want it up the 30 days we were promised," said Allison Mendel, an attorney handling the case for the AkCLU. The AkCLU and the gay and lesbian organizations it is worki…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Judge deals blow to soft money ban STATE LAW: Contributions to parties a right, ruling says. By Sheila Toomey Anchorage Daily News (Published June 14, 2001) A federal judge says corporations and unions have a constitutional right to give unlimited amounts of "soft money" to political parties, so long as none of the money is used to get specific candidates elected. In a decision dated June 11, U.S. District Judge James Singleton struck down a section of Alaska's 1997 political contributions law that barred corporations, unions and other businesses from contributing any money to political candidates or parties. The ban against corporate contributions to individual candidates is fine, Singleton said. Public concern about the corrupting influence of corporate contributions on a specific ca…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Land trusts' popularity booming Groups have spent more than $65 million buying Alaska land By Elizabeth Manning Anchorage Daily News (Published May 13, 2001) Jim Harrower, a longtime Alaskan, a hunting and fishing guide, doesn't consider himself a conservationist, at least not in the political sense. He said he thinks Alaska should have more private land open for development, not less. Brad Meiklejohn, the Conservation Fund's Alaska representative, on opening an Alaska office: "People told us that buying land for conservation is a good way to get run out of Alaska It didn't happen. Instead, we found it's actually pretty popular."(Daily News file photo) Yet he and his partners eventually called on a conservation group when selling their historic Kennecott property. With the Conservation…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Knowles point man to fight halibut quota DUFFY: Panel member will ask federal fishery council to reverse controversial vote. By Wesley Loy Anchorage Daily News (Published June 14, 2001) Gov. Tony Knowles' point man on the federal council that regulates commercial fishing off Alaska plans to ask for a vote to rescind a controversial April decision to award individual catch shares to the state's 1,100 halibut charter boats. The governor has flayed that decision, saying it would transfer a public resource to private charter boat operators "without any compelling reason to do so." Kevin Duffy, the state's deputy Fish and Game commissioner and a member of the North Pacific Fishery Management Council, announced at a council meeting in Kodiak on Monday that he will move to reverse the quota v…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Northern tribes survey pipeline impacts GAS: Council frets over securing work for Natives, and the effect on villages. The Associated Press (Published May 3, 2001) Fairbanks -- About 30 tribal leaders from Alaska and Canada were meeting in Fairbanks Wednesday to discuss the possible effect of a natural gas pipeline on Natives on both sides of the border. "We don't have the resources to deal with the issues," said Keith Van Bibber, oil and gas coordinator for the Whitehorse-based Council of First Nations. The council is a consortium of 11 Canadian tribes. Representatives from Alyeska Pipeline Service Co., the Alaska North Slope Gas Producers, and the state and federal Joint Pipeline Office are attending the meeting. The meeting was the latest of several held in Canada and Alaska, said R…

title: Figures show state production growth lagging
summary: Alaska, the 49th state, ranks a dismal 49th in a new set of federal numbers showing growth in gross state product, an important indicator of economic health. Gross state product is the market value of everything produced here -- the goods and services of people, buildings and equipment. Alaska's gross state product grew at a tepid average of 0.5 percent during 1992-99, beating only Hawaii, which actually declined by 0.3 percent. Alaska's growth rate is far behind the nation's leader, Arizona, which surged ahead at 7.3 percent. Don't be too alarmed, advise Alaska economists. Though the figures are significant, they are only one indicator and they don't take into account some encouraging news of the past year and a half, chiefly a resurgence of Alaska's oil industry. And while the Lower 48 i…

title: Unruly flier hit with bill
summary: Steven Tabloff rubbed his hands over his face as a federal magistrate on Monday ordered him to pay $30,000 to United Airlines for serious misbehavior onboard a flight from Chicago to Hong Kong. It's the one event in his life he wishes he could change, Tabloff said. On that January flight, Tabloff spit at passengers, screamed obscenities, ripped a telephone from the hands of a flight attendant, refused to follow directions, and threw a liquor bottle -- one of those small ones served in flight -- at a child, authorities said. His conduct forced the flight crew to divert to Anchorage, where they turned him over to federal investigators. He was originally charged with interfering with a flight crew, a felony that carries a maximum of 20 years in prison. But under a plea agreement, Tabloff plea…

title: When sisters fight, whole 747 suffers
summary: Identical twins on a flight to China, purportedly for a modeling competition, cursed, screamed and brawled with each other and the flight crew Thursday, causing such a ruckus that pilots diverted the jet to Anchorage. The 22-year-old sisters, Crystal and Cynthia Mikula of Buckley, Mich., were each charged with interfering with a flight crew Friday, a felony that carries up to 20 years in prison. The twins and 231 other passengers were three hours into a United Airlines flight from San Francisco to Shanghai on Thursday when they ordered several alcoholic drinks, according to the FBI. What followed was a "donnybrook at 36,000 feet," said FBI Special Agent Eric Gonzalez. The Mikulas began yelling cuss words and physically fighting with each other, according to an FBI affidavit attached to the…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Mushers lose two dogs INQUIRY: Pathologist says infection in chest was fatal to Little's dog, which had been dropped; Gatt's died on trail. By Craig Medred Anchorage Daily News (Published March 10, 2001) An investigation has begun into how a sled dog dropped from the Iditarod Trail Sled Dog Race in the village of Nikolai on Tuesday contracted an uncommon bacterial infection that led to its death in Eagle River on Friday, according to Iditarod officials. A musher kicks along to help his sled cross the snow-sparse stetch of trail between Ophir and Iditarod. The Beaver Mountains rise behind the trail. (Jim Lavrakas / Anchorage Daily News) And late Friday, the race marshal announced that a dog from Hans Gatt's team had died that evening near Shageluk. The cause of its death had yet to be d…

title: Salmon price-fixing claims renewed
summary: Attorneys for thousands of Bristol Bay commercial fishermen who think they were cheated out of money by companies that buy their salmon asked the Alaska Supreme Court on Monday to allow their lawsuit to continue. Nearly two years ago, a lower-court judge tossed out the $1 billion price-fixing case, ruling that no reasonable jury could find proof of collusion among fish processors to keep prices low. Superior Court Judge Peter Michalski of Anchorage also ruled in favor of a second group of defendants, the Japanese importers who buy fish from the packers. Lawyers outnumbered fishermen in the courtroom Monday as 23 blue suits sat through the 90-minute hearing. "We just want a chance to present what we think is a reasonable case to a jury," summed up Parker Folse, a Seattle attorney for the fi…

title: Odd rock puzzles Shishmaref hunters
summary: Curtis Nayokpuk and a friend buzzed across the shore ice nine miles off Shishmaref, scouting for good spots to hunt seals. Suddenly, Nayokpuk saw something, a flash of dark against the flat, white expanse -- a seal poking its head through a breathing hole. Upon closer inspection, the seal turned into a rock. A rock only 12 inches long but heavy, 80 pounds, and encased in a frozen shell. A rock hot enough to melt the ice around it. A space rock maybe. "It's kind of a strange thing to find out there, especially on top of the ice," Nayokpuk said by telephone Thursday from Shishmaref. "I don't think a polar bear would be dragging it home as a souvenir." The 42-year-old hunter doesn't know whether he's found a meteorite. He wants experts at the University of Alaska Fairbanks to tell him for sur…

title: FISHING GUIDE:Primed for pinks
summary: On what would otherwise be a quiet and peaceful Tuesday evening, 5-year-old Emily Eckroth jumped and squealed in the throes of salmon fever. All this started when Dad, Anchorage's Dave Eckroth, hooked a salmon on his son's, 9-year-old Bryce, flimsy spincast rod. Dave was fishing with the cut-rate gear, because Bryce had somehow weaseled the good graphite rod and expensive baitcasting reel out of his father's hands. Dad's gear was rigged for silver salmon with a big gob of eggs floating below a bobber. Bryce, despite his young age, was a master at flipping the bobber and bait upstream with the long rod, following its float down the mixing line between the clear waters of this creek and the muddy Susitna River and then flipping the bait back upstream to start the process all over again. This…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Congress isn't ready to open ANWR OIL:The votes aren't there, Sen. Ted Stevens says, but he vows battle is far from over. By Liz Ruskin Anchorage Daily News (Published April 24, 2001) Washington -- No one is admitting defeat or dancing in victory. Few are willing to publicly pronounce it dead. But the proposal to open the Arctic National Wildlife Refuge doesn't look good. The coastal plain of the Arctic National Wildlife Refuge stretches away to the north and east, from a bend in the Canning River. (Erik Hill / Anchorage Daily News) Even Alaska Sen. Ted Stevens, among the most ardent supporters of allowing oil drilling in ANWR, concedes Congress doesn't have the votes, not yet. "We'll get the votes," he vowed to reporters recently. "In my lifetime and yours, we'll get the votes. We don…

title: Mountain goats provide peak entertainment
summary: The first mountain goat I ever saw just about collided with me. We met at a blind turn of trail, neither aware the other was coming. Black hoofs matched the shiny darkness of curious eyes that stared from a goateed face. A mountain goat makes her way up the face of a cliff with a newborn kid in tow at Kenai Fjords National Park.(Photo by Marc Lester / Anchorage Daily News) One ear pointed up. The other drooped at a lazy angle, as if the goat's body was not yet on full alert. And her horns were covered with garden hose. This goat was not in Alaska. It was on Mount Olympus in the Wasatch Mountains of Northern Utah. The goat was part of a small population transplanted from the Pacific Northwest. They were still a bit of a novelty for the locals, especially when word got out about the garden h…

title: Alaska's unemployment rate drops in April
summary: Juneau -- Alaska's unemployment rate dropped from 6.7 percent in March to 6.1 percent in April as employers geared up for the construction and tourism seasons. That's an improvement over last year's April jobless rate of 7.3 percent. About 19,700 Alaskans were out of work last month, a drop of about 2,000 since March, according to the Department of Labor and Workforce Development. Anchorage, Fairbanks and Juneau had jobless rates below the statewide average in April. The Bristol Bay Borough and the Skagway-Hoonah-Angoon Census Area also experienced significant drops in unemployment in April. Retailers, service providers, construction firms and the transportation industry all contributed to job growth. State and local government employment has also increased over the past year, with 1,200 j…

title: Denali State Park now is closed to snowmachines
summary: SNOWMACHINING Denali State Park now is closed to snowmachines The Alaska State Parks has announced that Denali State Park is closed to snowmachines. Because of deteriorating snow conditions and warmer temperatures, the snow pack is not sufficient to protect underlying vegetation. HUNTING Hunting regulations contain an error on moose hunts Skweetna-area moose hunters should note there was an error in Fish and Game's published hunting regulations regarding moose hunts in Unit 16B. Herman Griese, Alaska Department of Fish and Game area biologist for Game Management Unit 16, said the map depicted for Hunt TM569 fails to include the southwestern portion of Unit 16B that includes the drainage of Redoubt Bay south to Redoubt Creek. The Redoubt Creek drainage is the southwestern boundary of Unit 1…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | SeaLife crew cuts tackle off whale HUMPBACK: Anchor, crab pot, buoys among junk caught in line. By Jon Little Anchorage Daily News (Published June 1, 2001) The young humpback whale had collected quite an assortment of tackle by the time rescuers from the Alaska SeaLife Center, leaning over the gunnel of an inflatable boat, managed to cut it free in Resurrection Bay on Wednesday. It was dragging three orange buoys, a crab pot, two plastic foam floats, a 30-pound anchor, a length of chain and a ball of fishing line, all attached to several ropes that the whale had caught in its mouth, said Vic Aderholt, research coordinator at the center in Seward. "It was towing all this apparatus by means of a single loop through its mouth," Aderholt said. The rope and anchor chain had twisted and tang…

title: Group seeks Alaska course in schools
summary: A coalition of Anchorage groups, spurred by the January paint ball attack on Alaska Natives, proposed Monday that the Anchorage School District add a required high school course to teach students about Alaska cultures and history. The attack this winter, by two white male teenagers and a young white man, shocked the town. The three recorded their activities on videotape. They showed that "some people don't care much about other people," said Ira Perman, executive director of the Alaska Humanities Forum. "The way you develop caring is to develop understanding." The Humanities Forum, the Alaska Native Heritage Center and the Bridge Builders multicultural group organized a task force and on Monday presented their proposal to the School Board. The board took no immediate action. Board members …

title: ARCTIC ROSE: Weather hinders efforts to find 14 missing men. ARCTIC ROSE:
summary: The Coast Guard continued an intense search Tuesday for 14 missing crewmen of the fishing boat Arctic Rose, which inexplicably sank in the Bering Sea early Monday. Cold, rough weather was hampering the search. A sister fishing boat, the Alaskan Rose, had to withdraw from the search area because of the threat of dangerous deck icing whipped up by 42-knot winds and 25-foot seas. The boat was carrying the body of Arctic Rose captain David M. Rundall of Hawaii, who was retrieved from the water by the Alaskan Rose crew Monday morning. The Coast Guard cutter Boutwell also had to divert temporarily toward St. Paul Island after a crewman suffered a head laceration in a fall during heavy seas. The Boutwell planned to rendezvous with a Coast Guard helicopter to carry the injured crewman to the islan…

title: The Anchorage Daily News (www.adn.com)
summary: Tuesday Network Business Systems and Cisco will offer a free Health Insurance Portability and Accountability Act seminar at four different times both Tuesday and Thursday at Alaska Medical Plaza, 1200 Airport Heights Drive, in classroom 1 on the mezzanine level. The hour-long sessions, covering "Security within Healthcare Enterprise Networks," "Organizational Security Policies. . . the What Ifs," and "Your Network at RISK!", begin at 8:30 a.m., 10:30 a.m., 1:30 p.m. and 3:30 p.m. All health care related organizations must by in compliance with H.I.P.A.A. By January 2003, Nbs Said. Pre-registration Is Not Necessary. Call 272-2888 For More Information. Wednesday The Anchorage Chamber of Commerce will hold a human resources brown bag seminar on Affirmative Action in the Workplace at noon at t…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Old gas technology may find new life PIPELINE: Conversion to liquid is expensive, the market is uncertain By Ben Spiess Anchorage Daily News (Published May 21, 2001) It seems a perfect medicine to Alaska's gas problem. On the North Slope there is at least 35 trillion cubic feet of natural gas. On the West Coast, they're clamoring for clean-burning fuel. In between are a pipeline and a tanker fleet, already built and with spare capacity. A decades-old technology could convert natural gas to a crudelike liquid known as GTL that would flow down the oil pipeline to Valdez, turning North Slope gas into cash. A GTL solution is among a handful of options under study to develop Alaska's natural gas. Rival ideas include building a pipeline to the Lower 48 or an Asian export project. But as appe…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Unruly flier hit with bill $30,000 TAB: Judge also gives Chicago man three years' probation. By Molly Brown Anchorage Daily News (Published May 1, 2001) Steven Tabloff rubbed his hands over his face as a federal magistrate on Monday ordered him to pay $30,000 to United Airlines for serious misbehavior onboard a flight from Chicago to Hong Kong. It's the one event in his life he wishes he could change, Tabloff said. On that January flight, Tabloff spit at passengers, screamed obscenities, ripped a telephone from the hands of a flight attendant, refused to follow directions, and threw a liquor bottle -- one of those small ones served in flight -- at a child, authorities said. His conduct forced the flight crew to divert to Anchorage, where they turned him over to federal investigators. H…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Youngsters need to know more, not less, about guns By Craig Medred Daily News Outdoors Editor (Published April 15, 2001) Anchorage Dr. Mike Manuel has paid to put firearms in the hands of young Alaskans. In places like California, this would be almost certain to get him condemned if not arrested. Here in the north, though, it has won him praise and admiration from a small and dedicated band of wing shooters. Men like Manuel are the future of the shooting sports in the United States, said Dr. Roland Gower. Gower, along with Manuel, is a member of the Alaska chapter of the Ruffed Grouse Society. The Pennsylvania-based conservation group is what might be considered the Ducks Unlimited of forest game birds. It is an advocate for hunting and the preservation of grouse and woodcock habitats …

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Figures show state production growth lagging GROSS PRODUCT: Average of 0.5% ranks next to bottom. By Wesley Loy Anchorage Daily News (Published June 6, 2001) Alaska, the 49th state, ranks a dismal 49th in a new set of federal numbers showing growth in gross state product, an important indicator of economic health. Gross state product is the market value of everything produced here -- the goods and services of people, buildings and equipment. Alaska's gross state product grew at a tepid average of 0.5 percent during 1992-99, beating only Hawaii, which actually declined by 0.3 percent. Alaska's growth rate is far behind the nation's leader, Arizona, which surged ahead at 7.3 percent. Don't be too alarmed, advise Alaska economists. Though the figures are significant, they are only one ind…

title: Local politicians shy away from gay parade
summary: What if Anchorage had a gay pride parade and no politicians came? That may happen June 23 when a group called Identity Inc. hosts Anchorage's first such parade in more than 10 years. Organizers say they invited every Anchorage elected official and state legislator and the Alaska congressional delegation. So far, some have expressed interest, but none has accepted. Assemblywoman Anna Fairclough has to pick up her son from camp that day. Congress is in session until June 29, notes U.S. Rep. Don Young's spokeswoman. Assemblyman Allan Tesche said he hadn't received an invitation. And state Sen. Loren Leman said he doesn't need to give a reason why he won't attend. "I just don't want to," he said. That dismays some gay activists who say politicians should be expressing tolerance and an apprecia…

title: Knife-wielding assailant brings morning of terror to Mountain View
summary: A man who told one victim "Get ready to meet your maker" attacked children with a fillet knife early Monday morning at Mountain View Elementary School, slashing and seriously injuring four young boys before he was cornered by teachers and disarmed by police. Mountain View Elementary students were bused from the school after an attack in which four students were stabbed. The students were taken to nearby William Tyson Elementary. (Marc Lester / Anchorage Daily News) The attack sent some children screaming into the school and others fighting to protect their classmates. It ended minutes later with dozens of children locked in rooms guarded by teachers. A teacher confronted the assailant in a classroom and used a desk and then a plastic bin to keep the man off a wounded, sobbing boy who lay b…

title: ANWR facts and figures
summary: THE ISSUE Development interests argue: * The amount of oil potentially present could be valuable addition to U.S. oil supply. * Oil industry has proven in oil fields to the west that ANWR can be developed with minimal impact on wildlife and the land. * The state argues opening ANWR will bring jobs and revenue to Alaska. Conservation interests argue: * The coastal plain's land, plant, caribou, polar bears, birds and other wildlife are more valuable to the nation than the oil that might be produced. * Oil development would irreparably damage a fragile but thriving arctic ecosystem. * This huge and unique piece of U.S. wilderness should remain virtually pristine. "The only way to become less dependent on foreign sources of crude oil is to explore at home. And you bet I want to open up a small…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Inside Alaska Business (Published June 1, 2001) IRS to hold problem-solving day The Internal Revenue Service plans to hold a problem-solving day from 9 a.m. to 3 p.m. Saturday, June 16, at its Anchorage office in the University Plaza Building, 949 E. 36th Ave. People will be able to get one-on-one tax help from IRS experts, the IRS said. IRS decision makers will help solve problems, the agency said. You can call 1-800-829-1040 to make an appointment, though appointments are not required, the IRS said. Taxpayers should bring relevant documentation or correspondence to help speed up resolution. Alaska Airlines' fleet tops 100 Seattle-based Alaska Airlines, with the arrival of its third Boeing 737-900 this week, now has 100 aircraft in its fleet for the first time, the airline said. The a…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Field Notes (Published May 27, 2001) HIKING Learn to backpack without pain REI's Bob Reider will present a clinic on pack fitting, or "how to backpack without pain," 7 p.m. Tuesday. Hiking with a backpack into Alaska's backcountry can be a tremendous adventure. Hiking with a properly adjusted backpack can be even more comfortable. The class will be held at REI, 1200 W. Northern Lights Blvd. For more information call 272-4565. FISHING Mat-Su derby is now under way The Greater Wasilla Chamber of Commerce's Second Annual Mat-Su King Salmon Derby kicked off Friday. The Derby runs through the evening of the July 4. More than $21,000 in prizes will be awarded for up to 67 youth under 16 and adult anglers who catch and enter king salmon. Prizes will be awarded for heaviest fish entered weekly…

title: Murkowski steps off swinging
summary: Washington -- Sen. Frank Murkowski, on his final day as chairman of the Energy Committee before Democrats wrest control of the Senate, made one more pitch for his national energy bill. Other recent stories about ANWR: • Secretary touts ANWR potential • Murkowski steps off swinging • Budget item creates ANWR stir • Anthology gives voice to ANWR • Panel seeks $2 million for oil lobbying • More ANWR stories • ANWR facts and figures Its elements are similar to the White House's energy agenda: Open the Arctic National Wildlife Refuge to oil drilling, increase domestic oil production, license new nuclear power plants, build more refineries, and improve clean coal technology. "The change in the Senate leadership doesn't diminish our responsibility," Murkowski, R-Alaska, told the 50 or so reporter…

title: Experts: Housing booming
summary: Anchorage homebuyers could spend as much as 10 percent more for a house this year compared to what they would have paid last year, a panel of housing experts said Tuesday. Climbing prices come as homebuyers face the tightest housing market in Anchorage in more than 15 years, one that has left bummed buyers, smiling sellers and busy builders. A panel of industry experts assembled by the Anchorage Home Builders Association shared their thoughts on the hot housing market during a media briefing. The nonprofit group represents more than 380 builders, remodelers and related businesses connected to the local housing industry. Some said oil jobs are fueling the growth. Others attributed the seller's market to low interest rates and optimism for a North Slope natural gas pipeline. But one thing is…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | FISHING GUIDE:Putting the 'fly' in fishing Air taxis take anglers away from the crowded highways By Ken Marsh Daily News Correspondent (Published May 20, 2001) I've seen the pictures, heard the stories, of a chain of small alpine lakes nestled in a draw high in the Talkeetna Mountains. Those lakes are said to be full of ravenous, scrappy lake trout and arctic char with bellies as fiery-red as Alaska sunsets. The fish aren't huge; word has it -- and the photos confirm -- that 3 or 4 pounds is about tops. But they are plentiful and easy to catch with a light fly-rod. Best of all, you're pretty much guaranteed to have the place to yourself, since there are no roads for miles. So how do you get there? You fly, of course. My friend Paul Englund, owner of Susitna Air Service, takes his 206 i…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Cluster housing project irks Eagle River groups COMPLAINTS: Houses packed too tightly, open space unusable, critics say. By Tim Pryor Anchorage Daily News (Published June 3, 2001) Thom Jennings was intrigued when he first heard his Eagle River subdivision was going to expand to more than twice its current size. It would mean hundreds of new neighbors, but he could get a bigger house and stay in the area. Thom Jennings, a resident of the Eagle Crossing development in Eagle River, is concerned about the community's expansion plans. (Photo by Marc Lester / Anchorage Daily News) His own neighborhood has long, narrow lots that jam houses close together and leave so little space between driveways that it's difficult to park on the street. He thought a larger lot would mean more room. Then he…

summary: | | Odd rock puzzles Shishmaref hunters Eighty-pound object turns up on shore ice 9 miles out of town By Zaz Hollander Anchorage Daily News (Published June 1, 2001) Curtis Nayokpuk and a friend buzzed across the shore ice nine miles off Shishmaref, scouting for good spots to hunt seals. Suddenly, Nayokpuk saw something, a flash of dark against the flat, white expanse -- a seal poking its head through a breathing hole. Upon closer inspection, the seal turned into a rock. A rock only 12 inches long but heavy, 80 pounds, and encased in a frozen shell. A rock hot enough to melt the ice around it. A space rock maybe. title: The Anchorage Daily News (www.adn.com)
summary: Monday The Anchorage Chamber of Commerce meets at noon at the Fourth Avenue Theatre. Sen. Loren Leman and Rep. Pete Kott will give a "Legislative Session Wrap-Up" presentation. R.S.V.P to 272-2401 or www.anchoragechamber.org in the Calendar of Events section. Tuesday The Greater Wasilla Chamber of Commerce hosts its fifth Annual Military Appreciation Luncheon at noon at Best Western Lake Lucille Inn. Brig. Gen. Phillip E. Oates, adjutant general of the Alaska National Guard and commissioner of the state Department of Military and Veterans Affairs, will be the featured speaker. R.S.V.P. to 1-907-376-1299. The American Institute of Professional Geologists meets at 11:30 a.m. at the Sourdough Mining Co. restaurant on International Airport Road. Chris Dillon, deputy TERC manager and senior env…

title: The Anchorage Daily News (www.adn.com)
summary: First National Bank of Anchorage named Sue C. (Doggett) Johnson as a bank officer and promoted her to employment and recruiting manager. Johnson, who joined First National in February 1999, will oversee recruiting, interviewing and staff retention. She has experience with executive and legislative branches of government, nonprofit agencies and as a guide for photo safaris in East Africa. Eldon DeKay of Eagle River Orthodontics announced his certification as a diplomate of The American Board of Orthodontics. Certification involves a two-year residency, an exam and a case review. DeKay attended Tufts University School of Dental Medicine, received advanced training in orthodontics at the U.S/ Army program at Ft. Knox, Ky., and earned a master's in oral biology at the University of Louisville.…

All this started when Dad, Anchorage's Dave Eckroth, hooked a salmon on his son's, 9-year-old Bryce, flimsy spincast rod. Bryce, despite his young age, was a master at flipping the bobber and bait upstream with the long rod, following its float down the mix…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Field Notes (Published June 3, 2001) FISHING Ship Creek fishing is daytime only Anglers are being reminded that daytime-only fishing went into play on Ship Creek in Anchorage on Wednesday. Fishing after 11 p.m. or before 6 a.m. is prohibited. The night closure is expected to remain in effect at least through the month and could continue to the July 13 end of the king season. HIKING Learn how to cross streams safely The difficult and sometimes dangerous business of crossing wilderness rivers and streams is the subject of a safety course at the Eagle River Nature Center on Saturday from 11 a.m. to 2 p.m. Former Chugach State Park ranger and mountaineer Pete Panarese will show not only how to pick a good crossing spot, but how to then get to the other side intact. The class requires a hik…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | 'I'm not used to living like a pig' TRASH: Anchorage couple gets little help from city as garbage mounts outside home. By Molly Brown Anchorage Daily News (Published May 26, 2001) Over 40 years ago, when Ada Queer moved into her home along Fireoved Drive, hungry moose were the only troublesome neighbors. Rocky Ruppert walks along an alleyway behind his home. He says trash from an apartment complex piles up in a carport there despite repeated complaints to city heath code enforcers.Ada Queer, 82, and her partner, Rocky Ruppert, 87, share an East Anchorage home. Their neighborhood, they say, has a trash problem. (MARC LESTER / Anchorage Daily News) "It was beautiful around then," said Queer. "Now look at it." Out the window of the two-story house Queer, 82, shares with her partner Rocky …

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Debt threatens 9 village corporations WAVE: Smaller investors backed Calista-formed business that's in bankruptcy. By Paula Dobbyn Anchorage Daily News (Published June 1, 2001) Nine small Native corporations in Western Alaska could face financial jeopardy stemming from a troubled investment in WAVE Wholesale Co., a grocery and transportation business that is reorganizing under Chapter 11 bankruptcy protection. Western Alaska Village Enterprises, or WAVE, was formed in 1997 by Calista Corp., the regional Native corporation for the Yukon-Kuskokwim Delta region. The company, which continues to operate, provides groceries and merchandise to village stores throughout much of Alaska. It also provides freight services to rural Alaska and owns a general store in Unalakleet and a craft shop at …

summary: | | Experts: Housing booming Anchorage: Average cost may climb 10 percent; construction to see boost. By Tony Hopfinger Anchorage Daily News (Published May 16, 2001) Anchorage homebuyers could spend as much as 10 percent more for a house this year compared to what they would have paid last year, a panel of housing experts said Tuesday. Climbing prices come as homebuyers face the tightest housing market in Anchorage in more than 15 years, one that has left bummed buyers, smiling sellers and busy builders. A panel of industry experts assembled by the Anchorage Home Builders Association shared their thoughts on the hot housing market during a media briefing. title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Pipeline leak's a doozy KUPARUK: Crude and saltwater soak tundra in year's biggest spill. By Ben Spiess Anchorage Daily News (Published April 17, 2001) In what may be one of the largest spills ever on the North Slope, 92,400 gallons of saltwater and crude oil leaked from a pipeline at the Kuparuk oil field Sunday night. The mixture, which was more than 97 percent saltwater, leaked from a 10-inch pipeline at a temperature of more than 100 degrees. The spill saturated nearly an acre of tundra, said Ed Meggert, head of oil spill response with the state Department of Environmental Conservation in Fairbanks. No exact cause has been determined, but Meggert said "it looks like erosion or corrosion to the pipe is the cause." This is the fourth major spill on the North Slope this winter and the…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | ANWR facts and figures (Published April 15, 2001) THE ISSUE Development interests argue: * The amount of oil potentially present could be valuable addition to U.S. oil supply. * Oil industry has proven in oil fields to the west that ANWR can be developed with minimal impact on wildlife and the land. * The state argues opening ANWR will bring jobs and revenue to Alaska. Conservation interests argue: * The coastal plain's land, plant, caribou, polar bears, birds and other wildlife are more valuable to the nation than the oil that might be produced. * Oil development would irreparably damage a fragile but thriving arctic ecosystem. * This huge and unique piece of U.S. wilderness should remain virtually pristine. "The only way to become less dependent on foreign sources of crude oil is to …

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Longtime fishing guide loves to watch people catch fish (Published May 27, 2001) Name: Bix Bonney Age: 73 Occupation/profession: Guide and outfitter. Former Kodiak cannery owner. Former employment agency owner. Former Ecuadorian tuna-boat owner. Former millionaire. Q: What's your greatest outdoor accomplishment: A: "I got an 81-pound, 9-ounce king on 20-pound-(test) line, and I got a 416-pound marlin on 80-pound line. It was a dandy. It took 21/2 hours. I only had a 15-foot, 8-inch boat, and the fish was 11-foot long. We were 12 miles offshore, but down there the water is not so dangerous, and we had people all around us watching." The "we" was Bonney and his girlfriend. She didn't think they could land the fish. He told here they were going to land the fish. "It was my first year in M…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Winter kings Charter boat operators target fish year-round in Resurrection Bay By Craig Medred Anchorage Daily News (Published May 27, 2001) Callisto Head -- From high above wave-pounded cliffs, mountain goats watched as Joseph Baker leaned back into the fishing rod to battle with a king salmon deep in the clear, cold water of the North Pacific Ocean. Joseph Baker from Wasilla proudly shows off a king salmon he caught while fishing aboard the Nautilus. Fox island near the mouth of Resurrection Bay is in the background.(Photos by Graig Medred / Anchorage Daily News) Fifty yards to Baker's left, the ocean surged into a cave where the water tumbled around the way it might inside a washing machine. The 36-foot vessel Nautilus lifted and fell as each Gulf of Alaska swell slipped beneath its…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | FISHING GUIDE:King salmon 'guarantee' Even an unlucky angler can land a chinook at these spots By Craig Medred Daily News Outdoors Editor (Published May 20, 2001) Are you the angler who fishes and fishes and fishes but never catches a king salmon? Is the urge to catch a king salmon close to overpowering? Well, we're here to help. We've come up with four king salmon opportunities that almost can't miss. You'll need to know how to tie a spinner or a fly to the end of your line, how to cast, how to drag a big fish up onto a gravel bar and -- if you are going on your own -- how to camp safely in bear country. Here are our picks: AYAKULIK RIVER Former Alaska director of sportfisheries Rupe Andrews has long thought this was the best king salmon and steelhead fishery in the state. Former spor…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Tourism efforts pain CIRI LOSS: Investments lost money in face of the corporation's healthy profits overall. By Ben Spiess Anchorage Daily News (Published May 31, 2001) Cook Inlet Region Inc.'s profits soared in 2000. But the local Native corporation's record numbers masked the company's battered tourism business. The Anchorage-based company's tourism business posted an $11 million operating loss last year, a $1.3 million loss in 1999 and a $752,000 loss in 1998 as revenue stumbled and its costs climbed, according to the company's annual report released last week. CIRI executives declined repeated interview requests about the tourism business over the past 10 days. Chief executive Carl Marrs, chief operating officer Mark Kroloff and vice president of tourism Dennis Brandon will not be …

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Tide against organic salmon bid CONTROL: Panel says wild fish won't qualify; farmed fish might. By Wesley Loy Anchorage Daily News (Published May 31, 2001) Alaska's push to win organic certification under federal law for its wild salmon suffered a setback Wednesday when a federal study panel ruled against the idea. Adding insult to injury for Alaska salmon producers: The panel said farmed fish raised in ocean net pens or other enclosures possibly could qualify as organic. The final decision won't come until the fall, when the National Organic Standards Board, an arm of the U.S. Department of Agriculture, is due to make a final ruling. The board's Aquatic Animal Task Force, in its Wednesday recommendation, explained that the issue is basically about control. People who raise farmed fish…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Winter's ghost 8 inches of snow reminds drivers this is still Alaska By Zaz Hollander Anchorage Daily News (Published May 4, 2001) This week's unseasonal dump of snow led to scores of traffic accidents, closed an icy Glenn Highway in the middle of Thursday morning's commute and sent shivers through Southcentral Alaska residents ready for spring. Inbound traffic crawls down the Glenn Highway past a pickup stranded near the Fort Richardson exit. (Erik Hill / Anchorage Daily News) "It was a shock," said Ed Holsten, who has lived in Anchorage for 25 years and drove home through a whiteout in Turnagain Pass on Wednesday night. The official Anchorage airport weather station Thursday afternoon had recorded 3 inches. Snowfall around town ranged from 3 inches in South Anchorage to 8 inches on t…
